name: Phobos
description: Phobos, in Greek mythology, is the personification of fear and panic. He is more than a mere abstract concept; he is a daemon, a divine embodiment of a powerful emotion. Phobos is most famously known as a constant companion of his father, Ares, the god of war, and often appears alongside his brother Deimos (Terror) on the battlefield, instilling dread in the hearts of opposing warriors. His presence alone is enough to disrupt formations and break morale, making him an invaluable asset to Ares' destructive endeavors. While not a major deity in his own right, Phobos' influence is significant, representing the psychological impact of war and the primal fear that grips individuals in the face of conflict. He highlights the psychological aspects of warfare often overlooked in favor of the physical, underlining the importance of terror as a weapon. His role is not just to inspire fear, but to embody it, becoming a living manifestation of the anxieties and uncertainties inherent in battle. Phobos' association with Ares solidifies his position as a key component of the war god's retinue, a chilling reminder of the emotional toll of violence.
subtitle: God of Fear

keyMyths.title: Phobos Accompanies Ares to War
keyMyths.description: Phobos consistently accompanies his father, Ares, into battle. His presence alone is enough to scatter armies and break the courage of even the bravest warriors. He doesn't fight directly, but rather spreads fear and panic among the enemy ranks, creating chaos and disarray that Ares can then exploit. This makes him an essential component of Ares' war strategy, more than just a follower but an active agent in the outcome of battles.

keyMyths.title: Phobos and the Shield of Agamemnon
keyMyths.description: In some accounts, the shield of Agamemnon is described as bearing the image of Phobos, along with other symbols of terror, such as Strife and Force. This depiction highlights the importance of fear as a weapon and suggests that even the greatest heroes recognized its power. The inclusion of Phobos on the shield demonstrates a conscious effort to intimidate enemies and gain a psychological advantage before the battle even begins.

keyMyths.title: The Horses of Ares
keyMyths.description: Phobos, alongside Deimos, is often depicted as tending to Ares' horses. These horses, symbols of war and destruction, are themselves fearsome creatures. Phobos' responsibility for caring for them reinforces his association with the tools and instruments of war, further solidifying his role as an integral part of Ares' war machine. This duty underscores the constant readiness for war that characterizes Ares and his entourage.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: An epic poem detailing the Trojan War, showcasing Phobos as a companion of Ares.
sources.text: An epic poem detailing the Trojan War, showcasing Phobos as a companion of Ares.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: A genealogical account of the gods, placing Phobos within the family of Ares and Aphrodite.
sources.text: A genealogical account of the gods, placing Phobos within the family of Ares and Aphrodite.

sources.author: Hyginus
sources.description: A collection of mythological stories, providing details about Phobos' role and parentage.
sources.text: A collection of mythological stories, providing details about Phobos' role and parentage.
